name: Facebook Marketplace - Monroe LA region free-pickup listing
address: Online - set location to Monroe LA, covers NE Louisiana incl Lake Providence and Tallulah
cost: Free to list; tires given away free or token 5 to 10 dollars each for usable passenger and truck tires
notes: Post two ads. First a FREE used tires you-haul for passenger and farm-truck tires, which clears in days to scrappers, budget drivers and DIY users. Second a separate FREE used tractor and ag tires you-load-and-haul ad for the cattle and hay crowd, mentioning silage and hay-tarp weights, troughs and feed bunks so the right buyer self-selects. Whole tractor tires go FREE not for cash. Bump the ad every few days and allow about two weeks for the biggest tires.

name: Local parish buy-sell-trade and farm Facebook groups
address: Online - search Facebook for parish buy-sell-trade and Delta farm and cattle groups; East Carroll Parish page about 4500 followers
cost: Free to post; tires given away free
notes: Local groups reach the exact cattle, hay and row-crop audience that uses these tires, and members are nearby so haul cost is low. Search names like Parish Buy Sell Trade, Tallulah Lake Providence yard sale, Delta Cattlemen, NELA farm equipment. About 74 percent of East Carroll Parish land is agricultural, so local demand is strong.

name: Cattle and hay producers - silage and hay-tarp tire weights
address: NE Louisiana and Mississippi Delta cattle and hay operations, reached via Marketplace, local groups, sale barns and extension agent
city: Regional Lake Providence Tallulah Bastrop Monroe Delta
quantity_limit: Producers often want dozens, can absorb a whole pile
notes: Tires are the classic free weight farmers lay over plastic to hold down silage piles, baleage and hay tarps - good weight, no sharp edges, easy to move. Whole car and truck tires are ideal; whole tractor tires work as anchor weights. Pitch as tarp weights to anyone covering silage or round bales. Caveat - in the warm South whole water-holding tires breed mosquitoes carrying West Nile and regs limit on-farm stockpiles, so emphasize active use laid flat on tarps or cut to drain.

name: Cattle producers - tractor-tire water troughs and feed bunks
address: Regional ranch and cattle operations, reached via Marketplace and local cattle groups
quantity_limit: 1 to several large tires per rancher
notes: Best home for the big tractor and earthmover tires. One large tire becomes a near-indestructible freeze-resistant cattle water trough by cutting one sidewall and sealing the bottom, or a feed bunk with one sidewall removed holding a couple hundred pounds of ration. Ranchers actively seek these because cheap or free. Advertise the large tires separately as great for tire troughs and feed bunks, FREE you haul; the rancher does the cutting.

name: Gardeners and landscapers - raised beds and retaining walls
address: Local - reached via Marketplace and parish groups
notes: Large tractor tires about 15 inches wide make popular stand-alone raised garden beds and stacked retaining and erosion walls; rammed-earth-filled tires stabilize embankments and ditch banks, relevant in Delta row-crop country. Smaller passenger tires go to hobby gardeners for planters and tree rings. Soaks up the odd large tires the cattle crowd skips. List as tractor tire for raised bed, planter or retaining wall.

name: LSU AgCenter - East Carroll and Madison Parish Extension office
address: Contact local parish extension office via Find Your Agent; main AgCenter line 225-578-4161
notes: The parish livestock and ag agent personally knows local cattle and hay producers and can connect you to farmers who want tarp-weight or trough tires. Call the East Carroll or Madison Parish office and ask who in the area uses tires for silage and hay covers or troughs. The 225 number is the AgCenter HQ switchboard, ask to be routed to your parish office. Not a disposal site, a connector to free haul-off demand. Phone is the verified HQ line, not the local office direct line.

name: Cut-sidewall and bunker-cover market - only realistic cash angle, low for one pile
address: Wholesale buyers and processors ship cut radial sidewalls nationally such as 24tons and KSI Supply, not a local public buyer
cost: Not worth it for about 40 tires
notes: Cut radial-truck-tire sidewalls and rings, about 24 to 28 pounds each, are a real product sold to dairy and cattle farms for bunker and silage covers, and a local farmer who covers silage might take your truck tires free to cut their own. But wholesalers 24tons and KSI sell cut sidewalls; they do not buy whole scrap tires from the public or pay you, and they do not pull from a small farm pile. Cutting your own sidewalls to sell is far more labor than the small return justifies. Treat reuse as free disposal, not a money-maker; the real cash is the landfill and by-weight ag-tire fees you avoid.
